News Briefing: Church in the World


Patrick Hudson, Ellen Teague, Bess Twiston Davies, Marko Phiri, Tom Heneghan 

                        06 June 2025, The Tablet
                    


Prev



Next





























The Lonza valley in Switzerland, where the village of Blatten was demolished by an avalanche. The local bishop cited Isaiah 54:10: “For the mountains may depart and the hills be removed, but my steadfast love shall not depart from you.”\xa0
Michael Buholzer / Keystone / Associated Press / Alamy



The Egyptian government denied that a controversial court ruling, declaring the land around the historic St Catherine’s Monastery in the Sinai Peninsula state property, posed any threat to the community which has inhabited the site since the sixth-century. \xa0
The Greek Orthodox Church reacted with concern to last week’s ruling, as the monastery had enjoyed protected legal status since it was built on the site of the burning bush. 
In a call on 30 May with Greek Prime Minister Kyriakos Mitsotakis, the Egyptian President Abdel Fattah al-Sisi said Cairo was “fully committed to preserving the unique and sacred religious status of Saint Catherine’s Monastery, and ensuring it is not violated”. Mitsotakis emphasised the importance of “preserving the pilgrimage and Greek Orthodox character of the monastery”. A Greek delegation was due to visit Egypt next week, the Athens government reported. 
Archbishop Ieronymos of Athens had called the court ruling “scandalous” and an infringement on religious freedoms.\xa0The Greek Orthodox patriarchates in Jerusalem and Istanbul and the Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople also called on the Egyptian government to respect longstanding tradition and agreements with the world’s oldest continually-inhabited Christian monastery. \xa0
The ruling came amid a government development project to boost visitor numbers to the region, which is popular with pilgrims and tourists.

The United Nations reported worsening humanitarian crises in Sudan’s civil war between government forces and the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces (RSF).\xa0The UN secretary-general’s spokesperson Stéphane Dujarric de la Rivière described dire conditions in the town of El Fasher and the Abu Shouk refugee camp, which are besieged by the RSF.\xa0 
According to the International Organisation for Migration, around 2,800 people, mostly women and children, fled the renewed fighting in last week in South Kordofan, as the RSF pursued plans to establish a parallel government in the Darfur and Kordofan regions.\xa0
The UN said a cholera epidemic in Khartoum State had worsened, with the number of suspected cases increasing by 80 per cent to over 8,500 in the last two weeks.\xa0Although the World Health Organisation has delivered some medical supplies to Khartoum hospitals, “much more is needed to contain the epidemic”, Dujarric said.

Church authorities in Kenya suspended operations in Kerio Valley after the killing of Fr Allois Cheruiyot Bett, amid reports of widespread insecurity across the Rift Valley region.\xa0
Bett, a priest attached to St Mulumba Church in Tot, in the Diocese of Eldoret, was shot dead by suspected bandits as he returned to the parish headquarters after presiding over a Jumuiya, or community Mass, in the valley. His death provoked protests in Eldoret ahead of his burial on 3 June.\xa0 
Among the Church facilities closed was the Chesongoch Mission Hospital, run by the Benedictine Missionary Sisters.\xa0Sr Rosa Pascal, the head of the mission, said dangers in the area was causing mental, emotional and psychological trauma to the sisters, making it difficult for them to carry out essential services.\xa0The mission employed around 100 people, including nurses and doctors, but many had fled the area due to the insecurity. \xa0
The Church said the closure was necessary to protect employees, as well as the sisters and citizens seeking treatment.\xa0The wider suspension was intended to pressure government into acting against the region’s insecurity.

Church leaders in African must speak out against the sexual abuse of women religious if they are to be “stewards of human dignity”, a Zambian nun said.\xa0 
“Sexual abuse represents a profound and hallowing failure to protect the most vulnerable members of our communities,” said Sr Linah Siabana in an address to the general meeting of the Conference of Major Superiors of Africa and Madagascar on 24 May.\xa0“We bear a collective responsibility to ensure that our conduct consistently safeguards the rights and well-being of those entrusted to our care.”\xa0 
Siabana, of the Missionary Sisters of Our Lady of Africa, who specialises in mental health work with refugees, said Church leaders must “move beyond protectionism” to support women Religious.

South Africa still feels the effects of apartheid decades after it ended in 1994 in the huge divide between rich and poor, Cardinal Wilfrid Napier OFM said last week.\xa0 
The 84-year-old former Archbishop of Durban made the remarks amid intensified international interest in race relations, after the US government granted asylum to a group of white South Africans who said they faced persecution.\xa0During a meeting at the White House on 21 May, President Donald Trump alleged to South Africa’s President Cyril Ramaphosa that a “white genocide” was taking place, a claim condemned by religious leaders and rights activists.\xa0 
“The structures of apartheid that were put into place cannot be reversed,” Cardinal Napier said in a news agency interview, though he noted the failures of black-majority governments to address the lasting socio-economic effects.\xa0“While racial segregation legislation has gone, removing legislation does not remove the reality,” he said.

Dozens of people died last week in a wave of attacks in Nigeria’s Middle Belt by militant members of the Fulani herder community. More than 40 people were killed in separate attacks in Benue State. 
Nomadic Fulani herders have long clashed there with settled farmers, many of whom are Christian, over access to land and resources.\xa0The Diocese of Makurdi reported that farmers were killed on their land by Fulani jihadists who fired indiscriminately on villages.\xa0
Fr Oliver Ortese, chairman of the International Advisory Board for Makurdi Diocese, criticised the failure of security services to respond to the first attacks in Jimba.\xa0“There is a military post where the Nigerian army personnel are kept by the government where this incident happened,” he said. “Were the soldiers sleeping while these shootings were going on?” \xa0
He reported widespread panic and “many residents fled their homes in search of safety”. Fr Ortese reflected: “They are creating humanitarian crises as those who survive are moved into camps where they become beggars to eek out a living. You cannot imagine the reality we live in here. This is horror, this is terror.”\xa0 
The Bishop of Makurdi Wilfred Anagbe has testified to the US Congress about religious violence in Nigeria. 
Elsewhere, at least 200 people died in floods in Niger State last weekend. The rural town of Mokwa, west of Nigeria’s capital Abuja and a major trading and transportation hub for northern Nigerian farmers, suffered the worst of the flood. At least 500 households were destroyed and two roads and two bridges collapsed after heavy rainfall submerged huge areas of the town.

Bishops condemned the killing of soldiers in Benin by alleged jihadist militias, decrying worsening violence in the country which has claimed thousands of lives.\xa0More than 50 soldiers were killed in April in government efforts to quell a longstanding Islamist insurgency.\xa0 
“The bishops of Benin express their deepest sympathy to the entire nation and to the grieving families of our soldiers who fell in the line of duty,” a bishops’ conference statement said. “We also pray for their brothers and sisters in arms who are still on the front lines, that the Lord may be their shield and their protection.”\xa0 
It continued: “Aware of the sacrifices made to preserve peace and security in our country, we pray for the eternal rest of these heroes.”

Churches in Sierra Leone issued guidelines to limit parish activities after the government confirmed the outbreak of mpox, a viral disease that has claimed the lives of thousands of people in parts of Africa.\xa0
The World Health Organisation announced in April that the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Uganda, Sierra Leone and Burundi had been hit by mpox, with thousands of deaths reported since last year.\xa0 
“As a local Church, we need to do everything possible to protect ourselves from the disease and also contain its spread by carefully observing the guidelines announced by health authorities,” said Archbishop Edward Tamba Charles of Freetown in a statement last week.\xa0 
The guidelines advise avoiding physical interactions, with the country’s health officials noting that the slightest contact such as a handshake could prove infectious. “I assure you of my prayers for God’s blessing and protection, especially from mpox,” Archbishop Tamba said.

Malta’s Cardinal Mario Grech called the shortage of vocation a possible “grace from God” that could lead to the laity helping administer the sacraments. \xa0
Speaking to The Times of Malta, Grech said: “Some are scandalised by these words, because it could prompt the church to recognise and utilise the diverse gifts present among all Christians rather than concentrating power solely within the clergy”. \xa0
Grech, the secretary general of the Synod of Bishops, said the Church must understand that the Holy Spirit was not the exclusive domain of priests, even if some clergy resisted the idea. While priestly ministry remains vital, he said, there is plenty the laity can do in the Church, outside administrative tasks. 
He cited an example from Switzerland where a lay couple effectively run a parish.\xa0As priestly visits are infrequent, the couple preside over christenings, funerals and weddings, instead of the priest. \xa0
Grech said the aim of the Synod of Synodality was to move the Church away from clergy-dominated leadership to give the laity more say in decisions made in parishes, dioceses and the Vatican.

Bishop Jean-Marie Lovey of Sion said, “I weep for Blatten”, after the village in the mountainous Valais region of southern Switzerland was swept under a bursting glacier softened by climate change.\xa0The Swiss bishops conference said it was “deeply shocked” by the disaster and urged the country’s faithful to support Blatten’s residents – who were safely evacuated beforehand – both materially and spiritually.\xa0 
In his message, the local bishop quoted God’s words from Isaiah 54:10: “For the mountains may depart and the hills be removed, but my steadfast love shall not depart from you.”\xa0
The frozen Birch Glacier above Blatten burst on 28 May, covering the village below with mud and rocks sometimes 100 metres deep. The 300 inhabitants were evacuated a week earlier when geologists noted the glacier was weakening.\xa0 
Several hundred thousand cubic metres of cliff above the village remain unstable with summer temperatures threatening further melting.\xa0 Switzerland’s glaciers, the most numerous in Europe, are expected to continue to thaw in decades to come, increasing the risk of landslide and threatening other mountain villages.

Adult baptisms in Belgium continued an upward trend seen in other European countries, but other statistics showed decline for a Church beset by secularisation and the abuse scandal.\xa0The 536 adults and adolescents baptised at the 2025 Easter vigil were 48 per cent more than in 2024, whose 362 baptisms were already an increase over 260 in 2023, official figures show.\xa0
But other indicators were less optimistic. There were 29,769 infant baptisms in Belgium last year, 5,057 fewer than in 2023, and falls of nearly 10 per cent in numbers making their first communion and 7 per cent for confirmation in the same period.\xa0Church weddings dropped by 6.5 per cent from 2023 to 2024, though a less sharp decline than the 24 per cent drop between 2022 and 2023.\xa0 
“Requesting and receiving the sacraments is increasingly a conscious choice,” said a message from the Belgian bishops accompanying the official figures.\xa0“For some time now, we have been witnessing a gradual transition from a cultural Christianity to a Christianity of conviction.”

The Cathedral of Notre-Dame de Paris, reopened last December after five years of reconstruction following its 2019 fire, dedicated one of its chapels to the Churches of the East.\xa0 Eight icons adorn the walls of the chapel officially opened on 28 May and entrusted to the aid society L’Œuvre d’Orient, based in the French capital.
“The cathedral is Catholic and therefore universal, open to all,” said Mgr Olivier Ribadeau-Dumas, rector of the cathedral.\xa0Quoting John Paul II, he said: “The Christian breathes with the two lungs of the Church, Western and Eastern.”\xa0
The icons, representing saints who founded the great Oriental churches, were blessed during a Mass presided by Cardinal Louis Raphaël Sako, the Chaldean Patriarch of Babylon. There are 23 Eastern Catholic churches that celebrate Eastern rites but are in union with the Pope.

The council of the city of Lisbon agreed to name a park after Pope Francis to commemorate his visit to Portugal in 2023 for World Youth Day. The 100-hectare Tejo Park spanning Lisbon and the municipality of Loures, in greater Lisbon, was created on a former landfill site as part of Lisbon’s preparations for the youth rally in August 2023. “The dream is for it to be a green park, used by people,” said Carlos Moedas, the Mayor of Lisbon.

Salesian missionaries in Haiti reported providing meals to 2,300 adolescent boys and girls daily to ensure they have at least one healthy meal a day. The order works with the humanitarian organisation Rise Against Hunger in three missionary centres across the island, including the Vincent Foundation centre near Cap-Haïtien which provides regular meals and allows young people to socialise in safety.\xa0 
Church workers have faced increasing difficulties in responding to the hunger crisis amid gang violence following the assassination of President Jovenel Moïse in 2021. One million Haitians – nearly a tenth of the population – have been displaced from their homes. \xa0Around 5.7million face acute food insecurity, according to the UN.

Pope Leo XIV appointed a Nicaraguan-born priest an auxiliary bishop of the Archdiocese of Newark in New Jersey. \xa0
Fr Pedro Bismarck Chau, who is bilingual in Spanish and English, was born in July 1964 in the Nicaraguan capital Managua.\xa0Ordained in 2008, Chau has parish experience in New Jersey, where he has served as assistant director of vocations for the archdiocese, as chaplain for pastoral ministry with the deaf and in missionary outreach to Latin Americans in the United States. \xa0
The Archbishop of Newark Cardinal Joseph Tobin said Fr Chau’s “deep faith, considerable pastoral experience, and love for the people of God will be a great blessing to our local Church”. An estimated 1.03 million Catholics live in New Jersey.

Archbishop Robert Casey of Cincinnati travelled to a town in rural Ohio to celebrate Mass with a community whose historic church was destroyed by fire. St John the Baptist church, built in 1891, burned down on 29 May. Officials have not determined what caused the fire but noted that workmen were in the process of replacing the church’s roof. \xa0
Casey, who was installed as archbishop on 3 April, led a Mass at the nearby Church of the Most Precious Blood the next day.\xa0“When we face times of trial, we know that God can take us on a journey from cross to resurrection,” he told the congregation. He recalled a parish in Florida whose church building was destroyed in a hurricane. They held Mass in the carpark for a year and realised “their true identity as people of God”.

Vatican officials joined delegates from Buddhist universities and monasteries in Cambodia and the Bishops’ Conference of Cambodia for the Eighth Buddhist-Christian Colloquium in Phnom Penh on 27 May. \xa0
Taking the theme “Buddhists and Christians Working Together for Peace through Reconciliation and Resilience”, around 150 Buddhists and Christians from 16 mostly Asian countries participated.\xa0
The prefect of the Vatican’s Dicastery for Inter-religious Dialogue Cardinal George Koovakad told the opening session that “reconciliation and resilience are deeply rooted in our respective faiths and are capable of building and sustaining lasting peace”.\xa0He suggested Christians and Buddhists share spiritual resources for healing people affected by war and injustice.\xa0 
Bishop Olivier Schmitthaeusler, the apostolic vicar of Phnom Penh, emphasised the peaceful coexistence between religions in Cambodia during his welcome address. He thanked the Cambodian government for its support of religious harmony.

The Catholic Bishops’ Conference of India (CBCI) joined the launch of the National Multi-Faith Action Coordination Committee, a national platform to support religious efforts to respond to “the cry of the poor and the cry of the earth” by bringing together multiple faith leaders and faith-based organisations.\xa0\xa0
The CBCI Office for Interreligious Dialogue and Caritas India were represented at the first meeting on 29 May. Alongside them were leaders of seven other faith traditions and representatives of various faith-based organisations working in India. \xa0
They discussed joint coordinated action in key sectors, such as public health and nutrition, water, sanitation and hygiene, child protection and education, and disaster risk reduction and climate action.\xa0The committee decided to hold its next meeting in Delhi to work out an action plan and to meet every quarter to monitor its activities.

Campaigners for international debt relief joined five days of action across 30 countries last week.\xa0They gathered at political landmarks including the UK Treasury, US Congress, and the United Nations headquarters in New York to light candles as part of a global “Relay of Hope” organised by Caritas Internationalis. \xa0
The event called for the cancellation or restructuring of national debt payments for countries unable to afford them, which affect more than 3.3 billion people who live in countries where the government spends more on debt payments than on health or education.\xa0 
The relay linked communities from Brazil to Cambodia and Puerto Rico to South Africa with relay “stages” outside the World Bank Office in Australia and in St Peter’s Square in Rome, as part of the #TurnDebtIntoHope campaign for the 2025 Jubilee.

News Briefing: Church in the World


Patrick Hudson, Bess Twiston Davies, Ellen Teague, Ngala Killian Chimtom, Tom Heneghan 

                        23 January 2025, The Tablet
                    


Prev



Next





























The star of the award-winning film Cabrini, Cristiana Dell’Anna (pictured), spoke at a screening in Geneva run by the Permanent Mission of Italy to the United Nations and the Permanent Observer Mission of the Holy See.
Album / Alamy



The Sodalitium Christianae Vitae (SCV) confirmed that the Vatican was to dissolve it.\xa0 The SCV, the largest Catholic lay movement in Peru, issued a statement on Monday confirming reports that Cardinal Gianfranco Ghirlanda had read a decree of dissolution at its general assembly.\xa0 
According to the reports, the decree cited abuse by the SCV’s founder Luis Fernando Figari and its lack of a founding charism. This followed a series of scandals in recent years, culminating in an investigation of the group by the Dicastery for the Doctrine of the Faith in 2023 and Figari’s expulsion last year.

The Vatican appointed pontifical delegates to run the Religious Family of the Incarnate Word in response to “severe deficits” in its governance and members’ continued reverence for the late Carlos Buela, who founded the missionary institute in Argentina in 1984 but was convicted of sexual misconduct in 2016.\xa0 
The Dicastery for Institutes of Consecrated Life and Societies of Apostolic Life announced the appointment of delegates to head its male and female branches in a document published on 11 January, signed by the dicastery’s newly-appointed prefect Sr Simona Brambilla MC. 
Sodalitium Christianae Vitae confirms Vatican dissolution order
Pope appoints Consolata sister as first female dicastery prefect

Bishop Rolando Álvarez of Matagalpa urged Nicaraguan Catholics to be “fearless and to maintain the energy needed to transform the world into a better place for everyone”. 
Speaking on 12 January during a visit to Nicaraguan priests and seminarians working in Spain, Álvarez said he encouraged young people “to be brave, creative and innovators” despite the repression of Daniel Ortega’s regime, which sent him into exile in Rome in January last year.\xa0He had previously been imprisoned for denouncing the excesses of the government in homilies and public statements.\xa0 
Days earlier, the regime revoked the legal status of the Foundation of Contemplative Dominican Nuns, the latest in a series of legal cancellations which have affected more than 5,400 religious groups and charities.

Church leaders said Haiti had yet to recover from the damage of the 2010 earthquake in which 160,000 people died.\xa0“It’s as if we are drowning; it’s about surviving,” Bishop Quesnel Alphonse of Fort-Liberté said last week, marking the earthquake’s fifteenth anniversary. \xa0
“People are beyond poor; they are living in misery and this affects the whole country,” he said, “there is the repeated phenomenon of families who lose everything in a single night, because the gangs arrive in their neighbourhoods and take all they have – they occupy their homes and force them to leave.”\xa0 
The Catholic charity Mary’s Meals said it was working in 500 Haitian places of education feeding 175,000 children every school day with support from local volunteers.

Pope Francis hailed the Cuban government’s decision to release 553 prisoners to mark the Jubilee Year as a “sign of great hope.” 
Speaking last Sunday, he referred to the news conveyed to him in a letter from Cuba’s President Miguel Díaz-Canel. A statement from the Cuban Ministry of Foreign Affairs described the prisoners set for release as “people sanctioned in due process for various crimes established by law”, saying they would be released gradually throughout 2025. 
It also observed that “as part of the close and smooth relations with the Vatican state, the Cuban government has maintained communication with Pope Francis and his representatives and, as in the past, has informed His Holiness about processes of review and release of persons deprived of liberty.” \xa0The ministry said this had led to the release of “more than 10,000 people sentenced to deprivation of liberty” between 2023 and 2024. 
In Spes Non Confundit\xa0(“Hope Does Not Disappoint”), the papal bull inaugurating the Jubilee, Pope Francis urged governments to implement measures of forgiveness and reconciliation.\xa0He invited them to undertake “initiatives aimed at restoring hope, forms of amnesty or pardon meant to help individuals regain confidence in themselves and in society.” 
Parolin sees ‘sign of hope’ in release of Cuban prisoners for Jubilee
Pope Francis proclaims Year of Jubilee themed around ‘hope’

The Texas Supreme Court heard oral arguments last week in Attorney General Ken Paxton’s case against the Catholic migrant shelter Annunciation House in El Paso, with significant implications for religious freedom and the Church’s ministry to migrants.\xa0 
A judge previously supported Annunciation House’s refusal to turn over documents about the migrants they serve sought by Paxton in February 2024.\xa0He counter-sued, alleging that the shelter “knowingly and purposely” sheltered undocumented immigrants, and urging the court to shut the organisation down. \xa0
Lawyers for Annunciation House cited Texas’ Religious Freedom Restoration Act (Rfra), which was enacted to protect religious organisations from government intrusions when they are fulfilling religious obligations, and also noted that most of the migrants in question have pending hearings about their asylum claims, meaning they are not in the US illegally. \xa0When the Texas authorities have a warrant for a specific person in the shelter, the shelter honours the warrant and the person surrenders to authorities.\xa0 
Texas has taken aggressive actions to combat illegal immigration in recent years, but this is the first time authorities have sought to shut down a Catholic charitable organisation.

The Spanish Diocese of Segovia voiced support for a priest who refused communion to two gay couples.\xa0It said the parish priest of the municipality of Torrecaballeros had “been obliged [in the course of] fulfilling his ministry and following the rules of the Church to deny communion to same-sex persons who live in the style of marriage – as can also occur with heterosexual couples who are unmarried”. \xa0
Rubén García de Andrés, the mayor of Torrecaballeros, wrote on social media that Fr Felicién Malanza had refused him and three other men communion “not due to our faith or actions, but for living with the person that we love”. The diocese said that to receive communion “some objective moral conditions” were required and that the Church could deny people communion “especially if to give it would provoke a scandal among the faithful”. \xa0
According to the Spanish daily El País, García de Andrés was barred from serving as eucharistic minister and reader in the parish two years ago.

The national auditor said the French arm of Caritas should limit its references to Christian charity to comply with the legal separation of Church and State.\xa0
A report from the Cour de Comptes (Court of Accounts) said Secours Catholique used biblical quotes and provided cut-rate rooms for pilgrims in Lourdes and Jerusalem. \xa0It also questioned a rule that bishops agree with appointments to leading positions. It suggested this contradicted a 2021 law reinforcing respect for official French principles, including laïcité – official secularism.\xa0 
“It is particularly appropriate … that the religious dimension of activities be strongly regulated and remain secondary,” the report said, adding that if not Secours Catholique might lose the privileges of a legal association of public utility. 
Secours Catholique declined to comment, but the smaller Protestant Aid Federation responded: “The spiritual dimension is what sets us apart. \xa0Like Secours Catholique, our project is to bring this spiritual vocation to life.” 
Pope praises ‘healthy laïcité’ on Corsican visit
Austria bids fond farewell as ‘compassionate’ Schönborn turns 80

The farewell Mass for Cardinal Christoph Schönborn, after almost 30 years as Archbishop of Vienna, turned into a national event with 4,000 guests in St Stephen’s Cathedral where Austria’s President Alexander Van der Bellen – a Lutheran – praised him as a “pontifex austriacus”.\xa0 
Schönborn, who turned 80 on 22 January, said he expected the Vatican to accept his resignation letter soon but did not know when.\xa0 No replacement has yet been named. He pleaded in his sermon for more understanding for refugees, a significant intervention just two weeks after the far-right Freedom Party was asked to form a government.\xa0 
“Compassion is what makes a society human. Mercilessness poisons society and ourselves,” he said. “My greatest wish is that mutual goodwill should never be lost, even when we have conflicts.” He bemoaned the contrast between the joyful thanksgiving at his farewell and today’s “widespread religious illiteracy” and the trend toward leaving the Church.\xa0But he added: “Where danger grows, so does salvation.”

Poland’s Catholic bishops threatened legal action against Prime Minister Donald Tusk’s government after it confirmed plans to reduce and downgrade religious lessons in state schools from September.\xa0 
“Since no statutory agreement on its content was reached with the Catholic Church and other interested religious associations, this is an unlawful act,” the bishops’ conference said in a statement. “We now expect the National Education Ministry to return to rule of law standards and refrain from further confrontational actions against believers who are full citizens of the Republic of Poland.”\xa0 
The bishops were reacting to a decree over the weekend by Poland’s education minister, Barbara Nowacka, confirming that religious lessons would be cut by half to one hour per week and confined to the start and end of the school day. 
Polish bishops threaten court action over ‘illegal curbs’  on religion classes
Polish Church told to act on abuse after claims against John Paul II’s secretary

A Church charity warned of increasing jihadist violence across the Sahel, citing an attack in Benin on 8 January that killed at least 28 soldiers.\xa0Maria Lozano of Aid to the Church in Need (ACN) told The Tablet that Al Qaeda-affiliated terrorist groups formerly entrenched in Mali, Niger, and Burkina Faso were expanding southwards to Benin, Ivory Coast and Togo.\xa0 
“This expansion reflects a deliberate strategy by Islamist extremist groups, which have advanced significantly since the 2010s, spreading their influence from northern Mali to new areas,” she said.\xa0The attack on a military post at the so-called triple border between Benin, Niger and Burkina Faso in which the 28 Beninese soldiers died was reportedly the bloodiest since the jihadist insurgency began in 2019.

Bishops in Ghana urged the newly-elected President John Dramani Mahama to curb illegal gold mining known as “galamsey”.\xa0The election campaign saw numerous public demonstrations calling for action against galamsey. 
In a visit to congratulate the President on his victory, a delegation of Catholic bishops reminded President Dramani Mahama of the need for urgent measures.\xa0“We plead that your government takes a serious look at galamsey and curb it,” said Bishop Emmanuel Koffi Fianu of Ho, the vice president of the Ghanain bishops’ conference who led the delegation.\xa0 
Ghana, the world’s sixth-largest gold exporter and second-largest cocoa producer, has suffered major environmental damage from galamsey. Once-thriving cocoa farms in regions like Western and Ashanti have been decimated, with over 47,000 acres lost to mining. Rivers have been poisoned by mercury and cyanide runoff from mining operations, threatening drinking water supplies for millions of Ghanaians.\xa0 
The former President William Nana Akufo-Addo of the New Patriotic Party promised to end galamsey in 2017, but failed to meet substantial success.

Archbishop Ignatius Kaigama of Abuja, Nigeria, said that “an unprecedented turnout” at a food distribution programme caused a deadly stampede at a parish before Christmas.\xa0Around 5,000 people attended an event run by the Society of St Vincent de Paul at the Holy Trinity Catholic Parish in Maitama, in the Archdiocese of Abuja, on 21 December, causing a struggle in which 10 people died.\xa0 
In his homily at a Mass for the victims on 12 January, Archbishop Kaigama said such desperation was symptomatic of the “heightened economic hardship affecting many families in the country”, and the parish’s efforts to help were a mark of “empathy”.\xa0He asserted that “no one should be allowed to die a needless death just struggling for what to eat”, condemning Nigeria’s systemic failure to provide basic necessities to its citizens. 
Deadly stampede at Nigerian parish caused by ‘empathy’ for poor, says archbishop
Bishops demand better for Nigeria ‘on brink of anarchy’

Catholic and Protestant leaders in the Democratic Republic of Congo launched a joint peace initiative to address the humanitarian crisis in the east of the country and across the Great Lakes region.\xa0The scheme from the Congolese bishops’ conference and the Church of Christ in Congo, titled the “Social Pact for Peace and Living Together”, was started to promote peaceful coexistence and social cohesion after three decades of regional violence. 
At the launch last week in Kinshasa, the authors explained that the pact was the result of a long process initiated by the Churches to encourage lasting regional stability. \xa0They urged African political leaders to halt armed conflicts, promote sustainable development, and curb exploitative extraction of natural resources.

A bishop emphasised the importance of harmonious relations between Sikhs and Christians in India’s northern Punjab state. Bishop Agnelo Rufino Gracias, the apostolic administrator of the Diocese of Jullundur, was responding to the launch of a Sikh youth group’s campaign to stop conversions and the establishment of new churches in the region. \xa0
“We are aware of the campaign, and it is the result of activities of some house churches and their ministries that have damaged the reputation of mainstream churches,” said Gracias. He said the campaign to counter conversions was “a wakeup call for all of us to keep intact the rapport we have made with the Sikh community”.\xa0 
Gracias, a former auxiliary in the Archdiocese of Bombay who was appointed to Jullundur in 2023, insisted that the Catholic Church “does not believe in forced conversion”. \xa0Christians make up just 1.26 per cent of the population in Punjab.

India’s bishops rubbished a Hindu nationalist leader’s claim that a former president was in favour of converting tribal Christians to Hinduism to stop them becoming anti-nationals.\xa0 
On 13 January, Mohan Bhagwat, chief of the Hindu paramilitary group Rashtriya Swayam Sevak Sangh (RSS), told a public function in Indore city in the central state of Madhya Pradesh that the late Pranab Mukherjee, who was President of India 2012-17, supported a campaign to convert tribal Christians to Hinduism, and had told him so in private.\xa0The Catholic Bishops’ Conference of India (CBCI) responded that “fabricated personal conversation being attributed to a former president and its posthumous publication with the vested interest of an organisation with questionable credibility raises a grave issue of national importance”.\xa0 
Describing the claim seems “diabolic, sinister, and not genuine”, the bishops said: “We do not believe Mukherjee made the statement because we hold him in great esteem for his contribution to the nation and the respect for the pluralistic, secular ethos of our motherland.”\xa0 
They questioned why Bhagwat did not speak about it before Mukherjee’s death in 2020.\xa0 “It is unfortunate that the thrice banned organisation with a history of violence is allowed with impunity to call the non-violent, peace loving and service-oriented Christian community as anti-national,” they added.\xa0 “Is it not the violent ‘ghar wapsi’ [returning home] programme of Vishwa Hindu Parishad [VHP – another Hindu group] and other similar organisations, curtailing the exercise of freedom of conscience of economically deprived tribals, the real anti-national activity?”\xa0 
The term “ghar wapsi” is used by the Hindu groups to refer to reconversion of Muslims and Christians to Hinduism, based on the belief that they were originally Hindus.

The Indian Supreme Court directed the Kerala state authorities to refrain from publishing or circulating data regarding the population, assets and churches of the Jacobite Syrian Church and the Malankara Orthodox Syrian Church, two denominations which emerged from the schism of the historic Malankara Church.
Justice Surya Kant, leading the two-judge bench, said on 15 January: “When you are asking the Supreme Court to intervene in religious affairs, we have to tread cautiously, extremely carefully … Today you [the two denominations] have come, tomorrow some other religious group will bring their fight to us … Should we intervene? The courts would ultimately have to take a very conscious call on this question.”
Earlier, on December 17, the court had ruled in favour of the a status quo in the property dispute between the denominations. The court was due to hear the case in detail on 29-30 January, considering whether the Malankara or Jacobite factions controls and manages certain churches, burial grounds, and related properties. 
Indian Supreme Court demands Orthodox factions obey ruling
Indian bishops defend ‘socialist, secular’ democracy

Church officials in Indonesia rejected claims from the Indonesian security forces that they had pacified the conflict zone in the province of Papua, expressing concern about hundreds of displaced people in the region. \xa0
Bishop Yanuarius Theofilus Matopai You of Jayapura and Fr Alexandro Rangga of the Franciscans’ Justice, Peace, and Integrity of Creation Commission Papua issued a statement on 17 January, saying that “an estimated 327 people remain displaced, with many others choosing to hide in the forest.” \xa0
They refuted the military’s claims that villagers had returned to their homes, and urged the Indonesian government to form an independent team to investigate causes of the conflict and ensure accountability for human rights violations.\xa0“Firstly, withdraw military troops from Oksop district and open up space for dialogue to find a peaceful solution,” they said.\xa0 
The resource-rich region of Papua has suffered unrest since it was brought under Indonesia’s control in a disputed UN-supervised vote in 1969, with frequent conflicts between the Indonesian authorities and indigenous Papuans.\xa0
The recent fighting between Indonesian security forces and pro-independence groups saw the military setting up posts inside church compounds and taking over community facilities. Fr Rangga said communities continued to hear gunfire daily and “fear of armed conflict prompted them to flee to safer places”.

Cabrini, the award-winning film about the patron saint of migrants, screened at the United Nations in Geneva last week as part of an event organised by the Permanent Mission of Italy to the United Nations and supported by the Permanent Observer Mission of the Holy See. Around 400 people attended the Swiss premier at the Palais de Nations. 
The film, which explores the founding of Mother Cabrini’s ministry with Italian immigrants in New York in the 1900s, was introduced by a panel including the lead actress, Cristiana Dell’Anna, the director general of the United Nations Office in Geneva and Archbishop Ettore Balestrero, the Vatican’s representative to the UN. \xa0
Sr Liz Dodd CSJP spoke about the Church’s work with migrants. Archbishop Balestrero drew the connection between Cabrini and the witness and ministry of Catholic Religious today, who continue to serve those on the margins.  
Housing the homeless – the parish that made a leap of faith
Liz Dodd goes looking online for an AI Jesus

The Governorate of the Vatican City State announced that staff with three or more children would receive a monthly €300 bonus at the Pope’s “personal initiative”.\xa0
A statement from the governorate – the Vatican City’s executive authority – said Pope Francis “supports large families and offers them financial assistance”. The bonus will apply until the eighteenth birthday of each child, or until the age of 24 if they are enrolled at university.\xa0
The Pope also extended parental leave for employees who are new fathers – including those who adopt or foster – from three days to five. Last year, the Vatican said it would open a crèche for centre\xa0for employees’ children aged between 3 months and 3 years old. \xa0Since 2020, it has run\xa0a summer camp\xa0for the children of staff aged 5-13 years old.\xa0Pope Francis confirmed last week that the governorate’s next president will be a woman.\xa0 
Speaking on Italian television, he said its secretary general Sr Raffaella Petrini will succeed Cardinal Fernando Vérgez Alzaga when he retires as president in March.

Umahi, Okpebholo Lament State of Edo Roads, Commend Tinubu’s Intervention





Published

            October 3, 2025                    


 | Updated

              March 6, 2026 at 12:27 AM            



By Richard Ogunsile  

Share:





























                    Follow Us on Google
                



 








 






The Edo State Governor, Senator Monday Okpebholo, and the Minister of Works, David Umahi, have expressed deep concern over the worsening condition of federal roads in Edo State, describing the hardship faced by motorists as “tragic and unacceptable.”
Naija News reports that the duo spoke on Wednesday during an inspection tour of the Benin–Warri dual carriageway bypass, where they decried the daily suffering of commuters, saying the problem reflects “years of neglect that can no longer be ignored.”
No Motorist Can Travel 100km Without Trouble – Umahi
Addressing journalists after the inspection, Umahi noted that motorists across the country continue to endure severe challenges on federal highways.
“President Tinubu met an overwhelming situation in terms of roads and bridges. You can’t travel 100 kilometres on federal roads without encountering serious difficulties. But the President is showing resolve, and Nigerians are already commending his efforts,” Umahi said.
The Minister explained that President Bola Tinubu inherited a backlog of abandoned road and bridge projects from past administrations, but has shown “courage, commitment and determination” to reverse the situation.
He cited commendations from respected Nigerians, including Arise Television Chairman, Nduka Obaigbena, as evidence that the administration’s road efforts are gaining recognition.
Levante Contract Terminated, New Firm Takes Over
Umahi also praised Governor Okpebholo for stepping in to address failed portions of the Benin-Warri road after the termination of Levante Construction Company’s contract under the NNPC Tax Credit Scheme.
According to him, the firm executed only four kilometres of reinforced concrete pavement in stable sections, neglecting critical portions despite repeated warnings.
“The contract was terminated after months of delay and poor work. We appealed to Governor Okpebholo to take over the first 23 kilometres, which he promptly awarded to CBC. The quality of their work is commendable,” Umahi stated.
He revealed that the federal government has directed contractors to reinforce the road base, elevate the carriageway, and ensure lasting durability.
Naija News reports that the Minister further urged the governor to extend his intervention to an additional nine kilometres, assuring that all federal projects under Tinubu are now designed with reinforced concrete technology to last “between 50 and 100 years.”
‘I Almost Wept’ – Governor Okpebholo
On his part, Okpebholo recalled his recent personal inspection of the road, where he was moved to tears by the number of accidents and stranded motorists.
“I almost wept when I saw the number of vehicles, including trailers, that had broken down or fallen on this road. The situation demands urgent action, and I am glad the Minister has joined us to push for solutions,” the governor said.

He stressed that durable infrastructure was not only vital for public welfare but also for the ruling party’s credibility.
According to him, although the concrete technology may slow construction, the project will deliver a lasting solution before the next rainy season.
“We are determined to fix this road. If I do my part and the federal government does its part, nobody will criticize the President. With more contractors on site, this project can be delivered on schedule,” Okpebholo affirmed.
Tinubu Approves 100km Coastal Highway for Edo
Later in the evening, Umahi was hosted to a special Independence Day dinner at the Edo State Government House Villa, Benin City, in celebration of Nigeria’s 65th anniversary.
At the event, attended by top government officials, former deputy governors, lawmakers, APC leaders and traditional rulers, the Minister made a major announcement on behalf of President Tinubu: the approval of a 100-kilometre stretch of the Lagos-Calabar Coastal Highway to pass through Edo State.
“Mr. President has directed that 100 kilometres by two lanes of the Lagos–Calabar Coastal Highway must now pass through Edo. This is a special gift to the people of Edo,” Umahi declared as he received thunderous applause from the gathering.
He explained that the state was not originally included in the project, but President Tinubu insisted on Edo’s inclusion as a reward for Governor Okpebholo’s loyalty and infrastructural drive.
Other states benefitting from the multi-billion-naira project include Lagos (100km), Ogun and Ondo (82km), Akwa Ibom (65km), and Cross River (27km).
‘Tinubu Treats Me Like a Son’ – Okpebholo
In his response, Governor Okpebholo expressed gratitude to President Tinubu, describing the approval as “a turning point in Edo’s infrastructure journey.”
“The President treats me like a son. Whenever we request support, he responds with express approval. This 100km coastal highway is proof of his love for our state,” he said.
The governor also showered praises on Umahi, recalling their brief time together in the Senate, where his first motion was on Edo’s bad roads, a matter he said has now found divine fulfilment with Umahi as Works Minister.
Commending the Minister in a statement issued on Thursday, October 2, by his Chief Press Secretary, Fred Itua, Okpebholo said: “He is the best Minister for Works Nigeria has ever had.”

                        Okpebholo, works minister bemoan state of federal roads in Edo                    



                                03 October 2025 5:28 am WAT                            




                                        By 
                                            Rapheal
                                        

 



































 

From Tony Osauzo, Benin
Edo State Governor, Senator Monday Okpbholo and the Minister of Works, David Umahi, were visibly moved as they lamented the appalling condition of federal roads in Edo State, describing the plight of motorists as both tragic and unacceptable.
During an inspection of the Benin-Warri dual carriageway bypass, the two leaders decried the hardship faced daily by commuters, noting that the situation reflects years of neglect that can no longer be ignored.
Speaking after the inspection, Governor Okpbholo and Umahi commended President Bola Tinubu for his swift intervention and commitment to reversing decades of infrastructural decay, even as they underscored the urgency of delivering immediate and lasting solutions.
The minister said that no motorist could travel 100 kilometres on federal roads nationwide without encountering major challenges.
He noted that President Ahmed Tinubu inherited an extensive backlog of failed road and bridge projects from past administrations, but stressed that the President has demonstrated courage, commitment and determination to reverse the situation.
“President Tinubu met an overwhelming situation in terms of roads and bridges. You can’t travel 100 kilometres on federal roads without encountering serious difficulties. But the President is showing resolve, and Nigerians are already commending his efforts.”
He cited commendations from prominent Nigerians, including Arise Television Chairman, Nduka Obaigbena, as evidence that the Tinubu administration’s road interventions are already resonating with the public.
He dismissed criticisms from some quarters, insisting that the President remains focused on delivering durable roads.
Umahi praised Governor Okpebholo for promptly intervening in critical failed portions of the Benin–Warri highway after the termination of a contract earlier awarded to Levante Construction Company under the NNPC Tax Credit Scheme.
He explained that Levante executed only four kilometres of reinforced concrete pavement in stable sections of the road, neglecting the worst segments despite repeated warnings.
“The contract was terminated after months of delay and poor work. We appealed to Governor Okpbholo to take over the first 23 kilometres, which he promptly awarded to CBC. The quality of their work is commendable.”
Umahi added that the Federal Government has directed the new contractors to reinforce the road base, elevate the carriageway and ensure no weak points are left unaddressed. He also appealed to Governor Okpbholo to extend his intervention to an additional nine kilometres, praising his willingness to collaborate.
He assured that President Tinubu’s road projects are designed with reinforced concrete technology to last between 50 and 100 years. He urged contractors to accelerate construction, working even at night, and directed the Federal Controller of Works in Edo State to provide photographic updates of progress.
While acknowledging funding challenges following the suspension of NNPC tax credit funding, Umahi revealed that President Tinubu has ordered the continuation of all such projects nationwide. He also hinted at a forthcoming federal intervention in Edo tagged “Coaster Highway,” describing it as a gift to the state.



Other News


Edo people score Okpebholo high on projects –CommissionerOkpebholo approves ₦2bn for Edo University infrastructureLocal Government Council’We won’t accept imposition of candidate of council boss, group tells Okpebholo
Governor Okpbholo, in his remarks, commended the minister for his prompt response to Edo’s infrastructural challenges. He recalled his recent personal inspection of the road, where he was disturbed by the sight of multiple accidents and stranded motorists.
“I almost wept when I saw the number of vehicles, including trailers, that had broken down or fallen on this road. The situation demands urgent action, and I am glad the Minister has joined us to push for solutions.”
The governor stressed that quality road infrastructure is not only essential for the people’s welfare but also critical for the ruling party’s credibility. He assured that the ongoing work, though slow due to concrete technology, will provide a lasting solution and should be completed before the next rainy season.
“We are determined to fix this road. If I do my part and the federal government does its part, nobody will criticize the President. For our people to trust us, we must act. With more contractors on site, this project can be delivered on schedule,” Okpbholo affirmed.
The minister and the governor assured Edo residents that the Federal Government remains committed to delivering durable and sustainable road infrastructure across the state and the nation at large.
Later that evening, a special Independence Day Dinner was held in honour of the Minister at the Edo State Government House Villa in Benin City.
The event, organised to celebrate Nigeria’s 65th Independence anniversary, was attended by top government officials, including former deputy governors, state and federal lawmakers, APC leaders, and traditional stakeholders.
During the dinner, Senator Umahi delivered a major announcement on behalf of President Tinubu, the approval of a 100-kilometre stretch of the Lagos-Calabar Coastal Highway to pass through Edo State, a state that was not originally included in the highway’s alignment.
Umahi announced to thunderous applause, “Mr. President has directed that 100 kilometres by two lanes of the Lagos-Calabar Coastal Highway must now pass through Edo. This is a special gift to the people of Edo.”
The project, one of the Tinubu administration’s most ambitious, is being handled by Hi-Tech Construction Nigeria Limited.
Other states benefitting from the project include Lagos (100km), Ogun and Ondo (82km), Akwa Ibom (65km) and Cross River (27km).
Originally, Edo was not on the route. Umahi explained, “But the President, impressed by your governor’s commitment to infrastructure, insisted that the state must benefit. It’s a reward for loyalty, leadership, and performance.”
 Governor Okpebholo thanked President Tinubu for the surprise gift, describing it as a turning point in Edo’s infrastructure journey.
“The President treats me like a son. Whenever we request support, he responds with express approval. This 100km coastal highway is proof of his love for our state.”
He also lauded the Minister for Works, recalling their brief time together in the Senate and how his very first motion was on Edo’s bad roads, a prophecy fulfilled when Umahi was later appointed Minister for Works.

                                02 October 2025 5:48 pm WAT                            




                                        By

Tinubu approves 100 km coastal road for Edo State


From Tony Osauzo, Benin
Edo State Governor Monday Okpebholo, and the Minister for Works, Engr David Umahi, were visibly moved as they lamented the appalling condition of federal roads in Edo State, describing the plight of motorists as both tragic and unacceptable.
During an inspection of the Benin–Warri dual carriageway bypass, the two leaders decried the hardship faced daily by commuters, noting that the situation reflects years of neglect that can no longer be ignored.
Speaking after the inspection, Governor Okpebholo and Engr Umahi commended President Bola Ahmed Tinubu for his swift intervention and commitment to reversing decades of infrastructural decay, even as they underscored the urgency of delivering immediate and lasting solutions.
The Minister for Works said that no motorist could travel 100 kilometres on federal roads nationwide without encountering major challenges.
He noted that President Bola Ahmed Tinubu inherited an extensive backlog of failed road and bridge projects from past administrations, but stressed that the President has demonstrated courage, commitment and determination to reverse the situation.
“President Tinubu met an overwhelming situation in terms of roads and bridges. You can’t travel 100 kilometres on federal roads without encountering serious difficulties. But the President is showing resolve, and Nigerians are already commending his efforts,” Umahi stated.
The Minister cited commendations from prominent Nigerians, including Arise Television Chairman, Nduka Obaigbena, as evidence that the Tinubu administration’s road interventions are already resonating with the public.
He dismissed criticisms from some quarters, insisting that the President remains focused on delivering durable roads.
Umahi praised Governor Okpebholo for promptly intervening in critical failed portions of the Benin–Warri highway after the termination of a contract earlier awarded to Levante Construction Company under the NNPC Tax Credit Scheme.
He explained that Levante executed only four kilometres of reinforced concrete pavement in stable sections of the road, neglecting the worst segments despite repeated warnings.
“The contract was terminated after months of delay and poor work. We appealed to Governor Okpebholo to take over the first 23 kilometres, which he promptly awarded to CBC. The quality of their work is commendable,” Umahi disclosed.
The Minister added that the federal government has directed the new contractors to reinforce the road base, elevate the carriageway, and ensure no weak points are left unaddressed. He also appealed to Governor Okpebholo to extend his intervention to an additional nine kilometres, praising his willingness to collaborate.
Umahi further assured that President Tinubu’s road projects are designed with reinforced concrete technology to last between 50 and 100 years. He urged contractors to accelerate construction, working even at night, and directed the Federal Controller of Works in Edo State to provide photographic updates of progress.
While acknowledging funding challenges following the suspension of NNPC tax credit funding, Umahi revealed that President Tinubu has ordered the continuation of all such projects nationwide. He also hinted at a forthcoming federal intervention in Edo tagged “Coastal Highway,” describing it as a gift to the state.
Governor Okpebholo, in his remarks, commended the Minister for Works for his prompt response to Edo’s infrastructural challenges. He recalled his recent personal inspection of the road, where he was disturbed by the sight of multiple accidents and stranded motorists.

Other states benefiting from the project include Lagos (100 km), Ogun and Ondo (82 km), Akwa Ibom (65 km), and Cross River (27 km).

In his remarks, Governor Okpebholo thanked President Tinubu for the surprise gift, describing it as a turning point in Edo’s infrastructure journey.
“The President treats me like a son. Whenever we request support, he responds with express approval. This 100 km coastal highway is proof of his love for our state.”

 Arts & Life 



November 12, 2025 by 
Osagie Otabor, Benin 




                    Museum that raises dust in kingdom                




 



   
What was meant to be a landmark cultural renaissance in Benin City has instead ignited one of Edo State’s fiercest debates. The Museum of West African Arts (MOWAA), built on the former Edo General Hospital site, promised to showcase heritage and scholarship. Yet disputes over its ownership, naming, and purpose—particularly between the state government and the Benin Palace—have turned a celebrated legacy project into a contest of history, power and identity, reports OSAGIE OTABOR
The Museum of West African Arts (MOWAA), sited on the grounds of the former Edo General Hospital in the heart of Benin City, is one of the most contentious legacy projects of former Edo State Governor, Godwin Obaseki. Conceived as a cultural and research hub, the project was designed to house the MOWAA Institute, Museum building, Rainforest Gallery, Art Guesthouse and Artisans’ Hall. While the entire complex is slated for completion in 2028, the MOWAA Institute and Museum building have already been completed and are set for public viewing. The initiative is projected to cost about $100 million, though reports indicate that only $20 million has so far been released.



However, the project has been dogged by disputes, particularly over ownership and origin. The Oba of Benin, His Royal Majesty Oba Ewuare II, contends that the idea was originally conceived as the Benin Royal Museum, an effort rooted in the longstanding struggle for the return of looted Benin artefacts taken during the 1897 British invasion. According to the monarch, he personally sought Obaseki’s support in 2017 to advance the dream of restoring the prized objects to the palace.       
He recalled that although the governor initially agreed to collaborate, events soon took a different turn. Oba Ewuare II said Obaseki later informed him that international donors and the Benin Dialogue Group were instead aligning with his administration to establish a different museum framework. The Oba alleged that, during the COVID-19 period, he discovered plans to divert funds and control over the artefacts away from the palace. He also criticised the establishment of the Legacy Restoration Trust and the involvement of foreign partners, particularly from Germany, whom he said acted without proper regard for Benin traditions or royal authority.



It was gathered that the Benin Monarch eventually withdrew his support for the museum initiative after reportedly sensing a lack of transparency in the activities of the Legacy Restoration Trust. Despite this, former Governor Obaseki pressed ahead with the plan, demolishing the Edo General Hospital to pave the way for what was initially named the Edo Museum of West African Arts (EMOWAA). The subsequent removal of the letter “E,” thus renaming it MOWAA, deepened public suspicion that the project was gradually shifting from a state-backed cultural institution to a potentially private-oriented enterprise.     
Funding for MOWAA, checks revealed, has come from a broad network of both domestic and international partners. These include the Edo State Government, the Nigerian and German governments, the Gerda Henkel Foundation, Deutsche Gesellschaft für Internationale Zusammenarbeit (GIZ), the Open Society Foundation, the Ford Foundation, and the Mellon Foundation, which specifically provided a $3 million grant targeted at training and professional development.






Read Also:\xa0Okpebholo takes EU ambassadors to monarch
In October, Governor Monday Okpebholo directed the Edo State House of Assembly to investigate both the MOWAA project and the Radisson Hotel involvement. The Assembly was mandated to determine the state’s equity in MOWAA, especially given the government’s financial commitment of N3.3 billion. Alongside the probe, Governor Okpebholo has reclaimed portions of the land earlier allocated to the museum.



Edo State Commissioner for Information and Strategy, Kassim Afegbua, explained that the government had to prioritise healthcare delivery over cultural infrastructure. He added that, were it not for the state’s investment exceeding N3 billion, the entire MOWAA structure would have been removed to restore the hospital. He stated that the change from EMOWAA to MOWAA first triggered doubts, especially as the reason provided—that “Edo” was not marketable—was deemed disrespectful to the history and cultural authority of the Benin Kingdom. The government, he said, ultimately adopted a “middle-ground solution,” curtailing the museum’s expansion and reclaiming part of the land, while planning to rebuild the demolished hospital.  Related News  We are working to resolve issues surrounding MOWAA, Okpebholo tells German government  Okpebholo receives report on MOWAA, Radisson Blu Hotel  BREAKING: Edo Assembly asks Okpebholo to take possession of MOWAA, Radisson Blu Hotel   
The crisis has also sparked public unrest. Various Benin socio-cultural organisations—women, youths, chiefs and guilds—protested at the Edo Government House and the palace, insisting that funds meant for the Benin Royal Museum were being misapplied. Speaking on behalf of the protesters, Osazee Amas Edobor and Osaro Culture Iyamu demanded that the museum’s inauguration be halted pending the Assembly’s probe, arguing that the dignity of the Oba and the heritage of the Benin people must be safeguarded.






Yet, not everyone views MOWAA through the lens of controversy. Independent artist and cultural heritage advocate, Enotie Ogbebor, argued that the museum should be celebrated rather than vilified. He said Governor Okpebholo’s committee would do better to shift from questioning ownership to promoting what he described as an “extraordinary asset” for Edo and West Africa. Similarly, the spokesman for the Edo PDP, Dan Osa-Ogbegie, criticized what he called the state’s sudden slide into “regression.” He argued that those calling for the demolition of MOWAA so that the old general hospital could be rebuilt were “misguided,” noting that the Obaseki administration had already relocated and expanded the health hub to the Stella Obasanjo axis, where the new central hospital complex is planned. To insist on bringing back the hospital to its former site, he said, was to place “vengeance above vision.”



“If Governor Okpebholo and his party feel uneasy about MOWAA’s governance, there is a civilised remedy,” he said. “They can constitute an independent panel of experts—heritage scholars, accountants, lawyers, architects, representatives of the palace, civil society and the media—to conduct a transparent audit of the project. Let facts, not falsehood, speak. What is indefensible is the bitter clamour to tear down a structure that elevates Edo’s prestige and Nigeria’s cultural standing.”



A source within MOWAA, who requested anonymity, also rejected claims that any individual or group was positioned to profit financially from the project. He said all contributors—government and private foundations alike—made donations, and that MOWAA was registered as a not-for-profit entity without shareholding structure. “MOWAA has a board with seats representing different interests. The Edo State Government has a seat. The Benin Traditional Council had a seat, previously occupied by the Crown Prince, until issues arose and it was agreed amicably that he stepped down,” he explained. “There are no dividends. MOWAA exists for public benefit.” He described the allocation of land and seed funding as standard practice for cultural and developmental investments. Preparations for the public opening on November 11, he noted, were already underway, with heads of global museums, ambassadors, and cultural leaders expected.






Responding to criticisms over the omission of “Edo” from the name, he said the change was intentional to reflect the institution’s wider regional scope. “We are a West African institution domiciled in Edo—not a museum solely for Edo art,” he said. “The location remains Benin City, but the vision is continental.”



However, Oba Ewuare II has maintained that MOWAA must be recognised in its original conception as the Benin Royal Museum, a vision he said was clearly articulated to—and supported by—foreign partners in anticipation of the eventual return of Benin artefacts looted during the 1897 British invasion. The monarch has therefore called on Governor Okpebholo and President Bola Ahmed Tinubu to investigate the circumstances that led to the rebranding of the project and to restore its original purpose and identity.



The Oba’s request has since received the backing of Governor Okpebholo, who publicly affirmed his willingness to align with the palace. Declaring the controversy settled, the governor described the project as a symbolic offering to the throne. “The issue of the Museum of West African Art is over,” he said. “It was a birthday gift to Your Majesty and it will be restored as such. I am glad that the Attorney-General, our political leaders and other stakeholders are here to witness this. I will take this video to President Tinubu, who has immense respect for culture and is a personal friend of the Palace. I am confident that Mr President will not support any act that undermines the dignity of the Benin Kingdom.”






The governor’s statement, viewed by many as a decisive turn, signals a likely restructuring of the museum’s governance and identity in alignment with the historical custodianship of the Benin Palace. It also raises expectations that the federal government may soon play a more direct role in resolving the dispute, especially as Nigeria continues to negotiate the restitution of its stolen cultural treasures. With the palace, state government and local cultural stakeholders now seemingly moving toward alignment, the future of MOWAA—whether as a continental arts hub or a restored Benin Royal Museum—may ultimately hinge on how both symbolism and governance are balanced in the coming months.

                    Okpebholo takes EU ambassadors to monarch                




 



   
Edo State Governor Monday Okpebholo has led a delegation of European diplomats to see the Benin Monarch, Oba Ewuare II, over the controversy surrounding the Museum of West African Art (MOWAA).



Some of the diplomats were inside MOWAA during its Sunday’s preview event when suspected thugs invaded the place and chased them away.



Addressing the diplomats at his palace, Oba Ewuare II urged the delegation to cooperate with the Senator Adams Oshiomhole-headed committee to look into MOWAA.       
He accused former Governor Godwin Obaseki of attempting to “mortgage” the rights of Benin people over their ancestral artifacts.



Read Also: Police, DSS, others begin patrol operations around churches in Abuja
He said Obaseki became angry with him after he refused to sign the documents.     
The monarch said: “It is like history wanting to repeat itself. My forefathers suffered during the British invasion of 1897, and it is not fair that this is happening again. The documents they brought for me to sign would have taken away our rights to these artifacts, and I refused.”






He insisted that funds were raised for the building of the Benin Royal Museum.



Speaking to reporters after the visit, Governor Okpebholo said he was shocked that he was not informed about the visit of foreign envoys to the state for activities related to MOWAA.



He said it was another example of “lack of transparency” that had surrounded the MOWAA project since its inception.






“We are here with the European and German Ambassadors, and their colleagues from the Netherlands and Belgium, to see His Royal Majesty.



They have spoken about areas of collaboration and also about what happened yesterday (Sunday). Honestly, I wasn’t aware such a gathering was going to take place; that’s the truth. However, we have spoken with the Oba and gotten his side of the story.



“The committee I set up will look into the matter comprehensively and make appropriate recommendations. There will be fairness.\xa0






“The controversy surrounding MOWAA stems from lack of openness by the previous government. It is curious how the project evolved from Benin Royal Museum to Edo Museum of West African Art (EMOWAA), and now to Museum of West African Art (MOWAA), without clarity or consultation with our revered traditional institution.”  Related News  Edo: A steady hand at the helm  Edo governor pledges to end blackouts through decentralised power initiative\xa0  Okpella stool: Sado remains Okpella monarch, says Okpebholo   
Okpebholo noted that the state government was never briefed on the project’s financials, ownership structure, or operational details, stressing that even the Oba of Benin had personally expressed concern about its handling.



“The previous administration never briefed me about MOWAA or handed over any documentation concerning the project. I know nothing about its transactions or agreements,” he said.






He condemned the demolition of the historic Central Hospital in Benin City to make way for MOWAA, noting: “Why destroy a functioning hospital to build a museum? We are proud of our heritage, but development must be responsible and people-centred.”



EU Ambassador Gautier Mignot said the mission discussed the MOWAA project and the unfortunate incident that occurred.



He said they exchanged views in a spirit of dialogue and partnership on how they intended to move forward.






Mignot said the European Union as an institution had no direct investment in MOWAA, ‘’but some EU member-states, including Germany, are partners in the initiative.’’



German Ambassador Dr. Annett Günther hailed Governor Okpebholo and the Oba of Benin for their hospitality, reaffirming Germany’s longstanding partnership with Edo State in migration management, youth empowerment and cultural preservation.



She said: “I am honoured to have paid a courtesy visit to His Royal Majesty, the Oba of Benin, and His Excellency, the Governor of Edo State.






“During my visit, I will attend the graduation of 450 youths from IT training programmes and visit several project sites. Our cooperation extends beyond development. It includes support for cultural heritage, including the return of over 1,200 Benin bronzes to Nigeria.



“On MOWAA, which is a completely different matter, we are very proud to be part and sponsor of this very unique museum. Not only museum, a campus, a centre of cherishing Nigeria and Benin heritage and culture in particular.



“To make this heritage part of our cooperation and available to the people of Benin City, Nigeria, including many foreign visitors.






“This will be an enormous attraction for tourism, for industry, for investors and for researchers, for architects to come together. But this, of course, this wonderful place of cherishing heritage and culture can only exist and flourish if there is a peaceful environment.



“This is why we regret the incident of yesterday (Sunday), but we are looking forward to a peaceful future of MOWAA and to a normal operation of MOWAA.”

                    Okpebholo, EU Ambassador, others meet Oba of Benin over MOWAA controversy                




 



   
Edo State Governor, Monday Okpebholo, on Monday, led a delegation of European diplomats to the Palace of the Oba of Benin, Oba Ewuare II, amid the growing controversy surrounding the Museum of West African Art (MOWAA).



Some of the diplomats were reportedly inside the museum during its Sunday preview event when suspected thugs stormed the venue and forced them to leave.



Addressing the delegation, Oba Ewuare II urged them to cooperate with the Senator Adams Oshiomhole-led committee set up to investigate issues related to the museum.       
The monarch accused former Governor Godwin Obaseki of attempting to “mortgage” the rights of the Benin people over their ancestral artifacts, alleging that Obaseki became hostile after he refused to sign documents that would have ceded those rights.



“It is like history wanting to repeat itself,” Oba Ewuare II said. “My forefathers suffered during the British invasion of 1897, and it is not fair that this is happening again. The documents they brought for me to sign would have taken away our rights to these artifacts, and I refused.”     
The Oba further explained that funds had already been raised for the construction of the Benin Royal Museum, which is distinct from MOWAA.






READ ALSO: Nigeria losing $15bn yearly to oil theft — Experts
Speaking to journalists after the palace visit, Governor Okpebholo expressed surprise that he was not notified about the visit of foreign envoys to Edo State for activities related to MOWAA.



He described the incident as yet another example of the “lack of transparency” that has trailed the MOWAA project since its inception.



“We are here with the European and German Ambassadors, and their colleagues from the Netherlands and Belgium, to see His Royal Majesty. They have spoken about areas of collaboration and also about what happened yesterday. Honestly, I wasn’t aware that such a gathering was going to take place; that’s the truth. However, we have spoken with the Oba and gotten his side of the story.”






“The committee I set up will look into the matter comprehensively and make appropriate recommendations. There will be fairness.\xa0



“The controversy surrounding MOWAA stems from the lack of openness by the previous government. It is curious how the project evolved from the Benin Royal Museum to the Edo Museum of West African Art (EMOWAA), and now to the Museum of West African Art (MOWAA), without clarity or consultation with our revered traditional institution.”



Okpebholo noted that the State Government was never briefed on the project’s financials, ownership structure, or operational details, stressing that even the Oba of Benin had personally expressed concern over its handling.





  Related News  CRPP vows to petition EFCC on Okpebholo’s projects  \xa0Governor Okpebholo moves to settle rift between Ossiomo Power, Chinese partners  Blackout: Okpebholo moves to settle rift between Ossiomo Power, Chinese partners   
“The previous administration never briefed me about MOWAA nor handed over any documentation concerning the project. I know nothing about its transactions or agreements,” he said.



He further condemned the demolition of the historic Central Hospital in Benin City to make way for MOWAA, noting, “Why destroy a functioning hospital to build a museum? We are proud of our heritage, but development must be responsible and people-centred.”



\xa0EU Ambassador Gautier Mignot said the mission discussed the MOWAA project and the unfortunate incidents that occurred.






He said they exchanged views in a spirit of dialogue and partnership on how they intended to move forward.



Mignot explained that the European Union, as an institution, has no direct investment in MOWAA but that some EU member states, including Germany, are partners in the initiative.



German Ambassador Dr. Annett Günther, in her remarks, commended Governor Okpebholo and the Oba of Benin for their hospitality, reaffirming Germany’s longstanding partnership with Edo State in migration management, youth empowerment, and cultural preservation.






“I am honoured to have paid a courtesy visit to His Royal Majesty, the Oba of Benin, and His Excellency, the Governor of Edo State,” she said. “During my visit, I will attend the graduation of 450 youths from IT training programmes and visit several project sites. Our cooperation extends beyond development; it includes support for cultural heritage, including the return of over 1,200 Benin Bronzes to Nigeria.”



“On MOWAA, which is a completely different matter, we are very proud to be a part of and a sponsor of this very unique museum. Not only a museum, a campus, a centre of cherishing Nigerian and Benin heritage and culture in particular.



“To make this heritage part of our cooperation and available to the people of Benin City, Nigeria, including many foreign visitors.






“This will be an enormous attraction for tourism, for industry, for investors, for researchers, for architects to come together. But this, of course, this wonderful place of cherishing heritage and culture can only exist and flourish if there is a peaceful environment.



“And that’s why we regret the incidents of yesterday, but we are looking forward to a peaceful future of the MOWAA and to a normal operation of the MOWAA.”

 The police in Edo have arrested a middle-aged man, identified as TJ, for allegedly killing his wife, Queen, in Auchi, Etsako West Local Government of the state. The suspect was said to have murdered his wife on Tuesday, September 30 with an axe and fled the scene after the incident. A resident, who spoke on condition of anonymity, told the News Agency of Nigeria that the couple had earlier celebrated the birthday of their two-year-old son before the tragedy. “There was no sign of a quarrel between them. We all went to bed that night, but by morning, neighbours didn’t see them come out and assumed they were still asleep. “It was their little boy who later came out of the room with bloodstains on his body.

 “When people saw the stains and noticed he wasn’t injured, they rushed into the room and found the woman lying lifeless in a pool of blood, her throat slit,” the source said.
